title: 1. Docker Introduction
definition: Docker packages applications into isolated environments called containers.
explanation: Each container bundles the app code, runtime, and all system libraries so it runs identically on any machine. Run docker --version to confirm the client is installed.
instruction: Check the Docker version installed on this sandbox system.

title: 2. Docker Architecture
definition: Docker uses a client-server architecture to build, run, and distribute containers.
explanation: The Docker client (CLI) sends commands to the Docker daemon (dockerd), which executes the instructions. Run docker info to see system details.
instruction: Query the Docker daemon for system-wide configuration details.
expected: Kernel version, OS, and container count are displayed.

title: 3. Your First Container
definition: Containers are created from container blueprints called images.
explanation: The docker run command creates and starts a container from an image. The hello-world image is Docker's official 'it works!' test.
instruction: Run the hello-world test container to verify Docker is working.
expected: 'Hello from Docker!' success message is printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker run hello-world

title: 4. Pulling Images
definition: Images are stored on registries like Docker Hub and pulled locally when needed.
explanation: Use docker pull to download an image from a registry. docker images shows all images downloaded on the current machine.
instruction: Pull the lightweight Alpine Linux image from Docker Hub.
expected: alpine image downloaded and listed under docker images.
hint: Run exact command: docker pull alpine

title: 5. Running a Container
definition: Running a container runs isolated application processes on top of your host OS.
explanation: The -d flag runs the container in the background (detached). The --name flag assigns a human-readable name. Here we launch Nginx, a popular web server.
instruction: Launch an Nginx web server container named my_web running in the background.
expected: A long container hex ID is printed and my_web is running.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name my_web nginx

title: 6. Listing Containers
definition: Docker tracks both running containers and stopped containers in its internal list.
explanation: docker ps shows active containers only. Adding the -a flag shows ALL containers — including stopped ones like hello-world.
instruction: List all containers including stopped ones in the sandbox.
expected: Table showing my_web (running) and hello-world (exited) containers.

title: 7. Writing a Dockerfile
definition: A Dockerfile is a text document containing instructions to assemble a custom container image.
explanation: Each instruction creates a layer. FROM sets the base image, and RUN runs commands during the image build process.
example: echo -e "FROM alpine\nRUN echo 'Hello from my image!'" > Dockerfile
instruction: Create a Dockerfile in the current directory with FROM alpine and a RUN instruction.
expected: Dockerfile file exists. Use cat Dockerfile to verify its contents.
hint: Run exact command: echo -e "FROM alpine\nRUN echo 'Hello from my image!'" > Dockerfile

title: 8. Building an Image
definition: Building compiles the instructions from your Dockerfile into a single, runnable container image.
explanation: docker build reads the Dockerfile and compiles it. The -t flag tags the image (name:version), and the dot (.) sets the build context path.
instruction: Build a Docker image tagged custom_app:v1 from your Dockerfile.
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 9. Layer Caching
definition: Docker caches build steps to avoid redundant work and speed up image building.
explanation: If a Dockerfile instruction hasn't changed since the last build, Docker reuses cached layers. Try running the build again to see caching.
instruction: Run the exact same build command again to observe the 'Using cache' speed.
expected: Build shows 'Using cache' on all steps and completes immediately.
hint: Run exact command: docker build -t custom_app:v1 .

title: 10. Volumes
definition: Volumes are dedicated storage systems for persisting container data.
explanation: Containers are ephemeral; their files are wiped when deleted. Volumes store data on the host machine independently of container lifecycles.
instruction: Create a Docker volume named db_data for persistent database storage.
expected: db_data is printed (volume name confirmed). Run docker volume ls to see it.
hint: Run exact command: docker volume create db_data

title: 11. Networks
definition: Networks isolate container communications and enable inter-container connections.
explanation: User-defined bridge networks enable containers to locate each other automatically by name via built-in DNS lookup, ignoring IPs.
instruction: Create a custom bridge network named backend_net for container communication.
expected: Network ID printed. Run docker network ls to see backend_net listed.
hint: Run exact command: docker network create backend_net

title: 12. Environment Variables
definition: Environment variables configure dynamic service parameters without modifying source files.
explanation: Docker allows configuration dynamically at runtime. The -e flag passes variables like port numbers or API keys into container processes.
instruction: Run a detached Nginx container named env_web with PORT set to 8080 using -e.
expected: env_web container started. Run docker ps to confirm it is running.
hint: Run exact command: docker run -d --name env_web -e PORT=8080 nginx

title: 13. Viewing Logs
definition: Logs output system stdout and stderr records from the application inside the container.
explanation: The docker logs command fetches background container logs. This is key to debugging startup failures and check web routing requests.
instruction: Fetch and display the startup logs from the my_web Nginx container.
expected: Nginx startup log lines are printed to the terminal.
hint: Run exact command: docker logs my_web

title: 14. Exec Into a Container
definition: Exec runs new processes directly inside a running container for diagnostic access.
explanation: Instead of launching a new container, docker exec runs commands inside an active container instance. Let's inspect the system date.
instruction: Run the 'date' command inside the my_web container using docker exec.
expected: Current date and time from inside the container is printed.
hint: Run exact command: docker exec my_web date

title: 15. Inspecting Resources
definition: Inspection returns low-level system metadata regarding Docker objects in JSON layout.
explanation: docker inspect displays configuration details. You can view mount paths, container environments, and assigned IP addresses.
instruction: Inspect the default bridge network to see its full JSON configuration.
expected: A JSON array with network configuration fields is displayed.
hint: Run exact command: docker inspect bridge

title: 16. Multi-stage Builds
definition: Multi-stage builds optimize production images by separating build tools from deployment artifacts.
explanation: Using multiple FROM blocks, you compile code in intermediate stages and copy only final outputs to the minimal runtime image.
example:
echo -e "FROM alpine AS builder
RUN touch /app
FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app /app" > Dockerfile.multi
instruction: Create a Dockerfile.multi file demonstrating a multi-stage build with two FROM stages.
expected: Dockerfile.multi exists with FROM alpine AS builder and COPY --from=builder instructions.
hint:
Run exact command: echo -e "FROM alpine AS builder
RUN touch /app
FROM alpine
COPY --from=builder /app /app" > Dockerfile.multi

title: 17. Stop & Remove Containers
definition: Stopping and removing containers frees up resources and cleans local filesystems.
explanation: docker stop stops the container processes gracefully. docker rm deletes the stopped container record and metadata.
example: docker stop my_web && docker rm my_web
instruction: Stop the my_web container and then remove it to free resources.
expected: my_web is stopped and removed. It no longer appears in docker ps -a.
hint: Run exact command: docker stop my_web && docker rm my_web

title: 18. Docker Compose
definition: Docker Compose manages multi-container applications using a single YAML configuration file.
explanation: docker-compose.yml defines services, networks, and volumes, allowing you to start and link multiple services with a single command.
example:
echo -e "services:
  web:
    image: nginx
  db:
    image: redis" > docker-compose.yml
instruction: Create a docker-compose.yml file with a web (nginx) and db (redis) service.
expected: docker-compose.yml exists. Use cat docker-compose.yml to read the file contents.
hint:
Run exact command: echo -e "services:
  web:
    image: nginx
  db:
    image: redis" > docker-compose.yml
